coredata.dc:title: Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer system based on ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs and PdAg nanosheets for the detection of 5-fluorouracil in serum
coredata.dc:creator: Jiao, Mengqi
Fan, Xiaolin
Wang, Zanyu
Wu, Kang
Deng, Anping
Li, Jianguo
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel electrochemiluminescence energy resonance transfer (ECL-RET) immunoassay based on ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs and PdAg nanosheets (PdAg NSs) was established for the ultrasensitive detection of 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) in serum. Exposure of graphene quantum dots by nitric acid etching of multi-walled carbon nanotubes to form ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs, which exhibited excellent electrochemiluminescence intensity. The prepared PdAg NSs have the characteristic of large specific surface area and low toxicity. Furthermore, ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs as ECL donor and PdAg NSs as ECL acceptor can directly carry 5-FU coating antigens and antibodies, respectively. Interference due to the introduction of other bridging molecules can be avoided. Based on the principle of competitive immunity, the 5-FU detection substance and 5-FU coating antigen will co-compete for the specific binding site on the scant antibody, realizing the efficient detection of 5-FU. Under optimal conditions, the proposed ECL immunosensor for 5-FU detection exhibited high sensitivity with a wide linear range from 0.0005 ng/mL to 500 ng/mL and a low detection limit at 0.17 pg/mL. This work took a simple approach to design the ECL immunosensor, the sensor had certain feasibility for the detection of 5-FU in the serum of cancer patients, and provided a new way for the detection of other small molecule anticancer drugs in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs
PdAg nanosheets
Electrochemiluminescence immunoassay
Resonance energy transfer
5-Fluorouracil

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and stability determination of the proposed immunosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We really appreciate the support from the Science Fund from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21175097, No. 31772053), the Project of Scientific and Technologic Infrastructure of Suzhou (SZS201207), the Supporting Funds for key research and development Project of the Ministry of Science and Technology (5010900122), the Suzhou Industry, State and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Novel Functional Polymeric Materials (SYG201636) and the Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. YX10900212).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM (B) HRTEM image of ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs. Inset: IGQDs in the ox-MWCNTs. (C) TEM image of PdAg NSs. (D) IR image of ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs. (E) XPS survey spectra of PdAg NSs (Ag 3d). (F) Pd 3d.

(A) EIS of (a) GCE, (b) GCE-ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs, (c) GCE-ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs-Ae and (d) GCE-ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs-Ae/BSA in 0.1 M KCl containing 2.5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3-/[Fe(CN)6]4-. (B) ECL curve of immunsensor (I) GCE/ ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs/Ae /Ab-PdAg NSs, (II) GCE/ ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs/Ae. (C) (i) UV–vis absorption spectrum of PdAg NSs and (ii) ECL emission spectrum of ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs.
Experimental conditions optimization of (A) PdAg NSs concentration and (B) concentration of 5-FU coating antigen and (C) concentration of 5-FU antibody.

ECL intensity of proposed immunosensor at different concentrations of 5-FU (ng/mL): (a) 0.0005, (b) 0.001, (c) 0.01, (d) 0.1, (e) 1, (f) 10, (g) 100 and (h) 500. (B) Linear calibration curve for the detection of 5-FU.
(A) 5 cycle scan peaks of the immunosensor at 0.001, 0.1, 10 and 100 ng/mL of the analyte concentration; (B) 18 cycle scans of the immunosensor at the analyte concentration of 1 ng/mL stability.
(A) Synthesis Process of GCE/ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs/Ae. (B) The fabrication of PdAg NSs-Ab immunosensor (C) Schematic illustration of the ECL detection mechanism for 5-FU.
Recoveries test of 5-FU in the spiked real samples.

head.ce:title: Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer system based on ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs and PdAg nanosheets for the detection of 5-fluorouracil in serum

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Key Lab of Health Chemistry & Molecular Diagnosis of Suzhou, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering & Materials Science, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Key Lab of Health Chemistry & Molecular Diagnosis of Suzhou

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Key Lab of Health Chemistry & Molecular Diagnosis of Suzhou, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering & Materials Science, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biology & Basic Medical Science, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biology & Basic Medical Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biology & Basic Medical Science, Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Mengqi Jiao and Xiaolin Fan contributes equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An electrochemiluminescence energy resonance transfer (ECL-RET) system using ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs as a novel donor and PdAg nanosheets (PdAg NSs) as the acceptor has been designed as well as applied in an immunosensor for detecting 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) in serum.

In this work, a novel electrochemiluminescence energy resonance transfer (ECL-RET) immunoassay based on ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs and PdAg nanosheets (PdAg NSs) was established for the ultrasensitive detection of 5-fluorouracil (5-FU) in serum. Exposure of graphene quantum dots by nitric acid etching of multi-walled carbon nanotubes to form ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs, which exhibited excellent electrochemiluminescence intensity. The prepared PdAg NSs have the characteristic of large specific surface area and low toxicity. Furthermore, ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs as ECL donor and PdAg NSs as ECL acceptor can directly carry 5-FU coating antigens and antibodies, respectively. Interference due to the introduction of other bridging molecules can be avoided. Based on the principle of competitive immunity, the 5-FU detection substance and 5-FU coating antigen will co-compete for the specific binding site on the scant antibody, realizing the efficient detection of 5-FU. Under optimal conditions, the proposed ECL immunosensor for 5-FU detection exhibited high sensitivity with a wide linear range from 0.0005 ng/mL to 500 ng/mL and a low detection limit at 0.17 pg/mL. This work took a simple approach to design the ECL immunosensor, the sensor had certain feasibility for the detection of 5-FU in the serum of cancer patients, and provided a new way for the detection of other small molecule anticancer drugs in the future.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU), a potent chemotherapeutic agent of the fluoropyrimidine class and the third most common chemotherapeutic agent, is widely used in the treatment of solid malignancies, including head and neck and gastrointestinal tumors [1,2]. Overuse of this drug may cause adverse reactions in the body, and known complications include acute myocardial infarction, heart failure or arrhythmia. There are no trivial matters in front of life. These side effects and potential dangers that have appeared remind us that we need to be more cautious about the clinical use of such drugs. 5-FU is a double-edged sword, with advantages and disadvantages, and reasonable use is particularly important. Its detection is also extremely important.

At present, the commonly used detection methods for 5-FU are mainly high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3], liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [4], gas chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (GC–MS/MS) [5]. The above detection methods have the advantages of high resolution and high sensitivity, but there are some limitations in practical application, such as relatively complicated sample pretreatment and relatively expensive detection equipment. The development of a simple and efficient detection and analysis method has practical significance for practical applications and other fields.

Electrochemiluminescence immunoassay (ECLIA) is a valuable combination of electrochemiluminescence and immunoassay, which combines the various advantages of electrochemiluminescence and immunology, and therefore has many unique advantages. Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET) strategy has attracted increasing attention as an energy transfer strategy in the field of biosensors [6–8]. The material that provides energy is called the donor and the material that receives energy is called the acceptor. Under the excitation of a certain electric field, the acceptor can quench the energy of the donor. Two basic conditions for ECL-RET are that the distance between the donor and the acceptor is close enough and the ECL emission spectrum of the donor and the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of the acceptor can overlap effectively [9,10]. In this work, we hope to construct an ECL-RET immunosensor based on specific recognition between two nanomaterials and 5-FU coating antigens and antibodies for the identification and quantitative detection of 5-FU targets. The selection of luminophores is very important for the construction of sensors [11,12]. During the exploration process, many new and efficient luminophores have been reported in many literatures. Among them, quantum dots (QDs) not only have high luminous efficiency and good stability, but also can be adjusted in size according to requirements [13,14]. QDs have become the most widely used ECL luminophores and a good choice for the construction of immunosensors [15–17]. In recent decades, the application of cadmium Cd-based and Pb-based quantum dots in electrochemical analysis is relatively mature [18,19]. But both Cd and Pb are heavy metal elements, they are highly toxic and will damage biological activity substances to a certain extent, which is not conducive to the application of biosensors [20]. In recent years, researchers have never stopped working in the field of quantum dot luminophores. Besides strong ECL emission, the non-toxic characteristics of new quantum dots are also very important. Quantum dots (GQDs) have become a series of new nano-materials because of their small size, good stability, high biocompatibility and excellent catalysis, which has attracted the attention of researchers [21–24]. In 2004, Scrivens et al. first obtained luminophore GQDs with three colors (green–blue, yellow, and orange) by etching single-walled carbon nanotubes with 3.3 M nitric acid for 48 h [25]. In 2019, Chi et al. discovered by chance that nitric acid etched multi-walled carbon nanotubes to form oxidized multi-walled carbon nanotubes and etched graphene quantum dots (ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs) with strong ECL emission [26]. A simple method was used to synthesize nontoxic ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs, which exhibited good stability and high luminous efficiency. In the ECL-RET system, ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs are a good choice as the donor material.

In the research of ECL-RET, in addition to the selection of donor materials, the properties of acceptor materials are also the factors that determine the excellent performance of immunosensors. At present, many studies on acceptor materials have found that noble metal nanoparticles have good electrical conductivity and can adjust the UV absorption according to the needs. They are often used as acceptor [27], and are widely used in electrochemistry. Besides, some 3D nanomaterials, such as nanosheets, nanoparticles and even some composite nanomaterials [28–30] are also very good choices. PdAg nanosheets (PdAg NSs) have excellent characteristics such as large specific surface area and low toxicity, and they are spread on the electrode surface as a base material. The three-dimensional structure of PdAg NSs has an ultra-thin thickness, and the combination with zero-dimensional quantum dots can reduce the distance between the donor and the acceptor to a certain extent, thereby enhancing the quenching effect of the acceptor on the donor [31]. Besides, PdAg NSs can directly connect antibodies through Pd-N bonds [32], which simplifies the construction process of the sensor. Therefore, PdAg NSs was chosen as the acceptor for the ECL-RET system.

In this work, a novel competitive ECL immunosensor based on energy resonance transfer was constructed. In this ECL-RET system, non-toxic ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs with high ECL luminescence efficiency was used as energy donor for the first time, and PdAg NSs was used as acceptor to label antibodies. The absorption spectra of PdAg NSs can perfectly overlap with the ECL spectra of the donor. The structure of PdAg NSs has a good specific surface area, which can connect more antibodies. The ECL immunosensor assembled based on the above strategy had a wide detection range and low detection limit for the detection of 5-FU. It provides a feasible analytical method for the detection of 5-FU content in the blood or urine of cancer patients and similar small molecules.
The reagents and apparatus involved in the experiment were supplied described in the Supplementary material.

The preparation method of ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs refers to the previously reported literature [26]. Nitric acid was first diluted to 7.5 M, and then 30 mg of MWCNTs was mixed with 30 mL of 7.5 M HNO3 under stirring and refluxing in an oil bath at 140 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained suspension was diluted with water to 100 mL, suction filtered through a 0.2 μm filter membrane, and washed with water for several times until the filtrate became neutral. Finally, the ox-MWCNTs suspension was obtained after redispersing the purified ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs in 10 mL of water.

The preparation method of PdAg NSs according to the procedure reported [33]. Initially, 10 mg Na2PdCl4 and 30 mg W(CO)6 were mixed in a vial, then 8 mL dimethylformamide (DMF) and 2 mL acetic acid were added. The mixture was sonicated until a clear solution was obtained, and the solution was heated in a 140 °C oil bath for 2 h. Then cooled to room temperature, the product was washed with ethanol and water by centrifugation, and the obtained Pd nanosheets were dissolved in 5 mL of ethylene glycol (EG) for subsequent use. Secondly, 25 mg of ascorbic acid (AA) and 25 mg of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) were added to the above EG solution. The mixture was sonicated until a clear solution was obtained. Finally, the above mixture was heated, and when the temperature was increased to 160 °C, the ethylene glycol solution of AgNO3 was quickly poured into the mixture to continue the reaction for 1 h. Then the product was washed with ethanol and deionized water after centrifugation and dried at 50 °C to obtain PdAg NSs.

The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was washed with deionized water and ethanol by using ultrasound for 30 s, respectively. Then, the GCE was treated with 30 nm α-Al2O3 polishing powder, and was rinsed several times with deionized water to obtain a clean GCE. The first step was the assembly of the immunosensor substrate, as shown in Scheme 1 A. First, the prepared ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs was activated with EDC and NHS. 10 μL of the above solution was added to the surface of GCE and naturally dried in the air. Then 10 μL 5-FU coating antigen solution (8 μg/mL) was dropped on the surface of the above electrodes, the coating antigen could be linked to the carboxyl group on the surface of ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs via amide bond. Electrodes were incubated overnight in refrigerator at 4 °C. On the next day, 10 μL of BSA (5 %) was dropped onto the electrode surface to block the nonspecific binding sites on the antigen for 1 h. Finally, the electrode was washed with PBS (0.01 M) to remove the unconnected antigens and BSA, and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Assembly of the immune probe was shown in Scheme 1B. 10 μL of PdAg NSs was added to the centrifuge tube, then, 10 μL of 5 μg/mL 5-FU antibody was added to the above solution, mixed gently, and placed in refrigerator at 4 °C to incubate overnight. Similarly, 10 μL of BSA (5 %) was added to the centrifuge tube for 1 h to block non-specific binding sites, and finally PdAg NSs-Ab probe was obtained by centrifugating and washing to remove unbound antibodies and BSA. Finally, the PdAg NSs-Ab probe was modified on the surface of the immune substrate and incubated for 1 h, then the electrode was rinsed with 0.01 M PBS buffer solution to remove the excess probe.

The ECL detection mechanism for 5-FU was shown in Scheme 1C. The electrode was placed in PBS containing of 0.1 M K2S2O8 and ECL measurement was performed. In the absence of 5-FU, the PdAg NSs-Ab probe can specifically recognize ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs-Ae on the electrode, and the ECL-RET efficiency was high and the ECL signal was low at this time. In the presence of the 5-FU, the PdAg NSs-Ab probe can bind to the 5-FU target, and after incubation, it will be washed away by PBS, showing a strong ECL signal. Different concentrations of 5-FU target have different ECL signal intensity, and the quantitative detection of 5-FU target substance was realized according to the ECL signal.

The morphology of ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM), as shown in the Fig. 1 A, the TEM image showed that many short tubular structures had been successfully synthesized. The elemental distribution was uncovered by STEM-EDS elemental mappings (Fig. S1), where the C and O were distributed uniformly throughout the nanotubes. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1B) clearly showed that there were some broken sites on the tubular structure where immobilized GQDs (IGQDs) with graphitic lattice can be found. The IGQDs had distinct lattice structures with lattice spacings of 0.344 nm (inset of Fig. 1B), corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of GQDs [34]. In addition, infrared characterization was also carried out (Fig. 1D), we could clearly see the vibration at 1600 cm−1, compared with the literature [35], which was assigned as carbonyl group. The vibration of about 3300 cm−1 can generally be referred to as a hydroxyl group, which indicated that there are carboxyl groups in the material. The combination of the above two vibrations indicated that there are carboxyl groups in the material, which could better connect antigens, while avoiding the adverse effects of introducing other cross-linking molecules on the system. According to the above characterization, ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs was synthesized successfully.

The morphologies of PdAg NSs were characterized by TEM. As shown in the Fig. 1C, the TEM images showed that the typical 3D nanosheet morphology assembled from many 2D thinner PdAg NSs. STEM-EDS elemental mappings demonstrated the uniform distribution of Ag and Pd elements (Fig. S2). The nanosheet had a larger specific surface area and could load more 5-FU antigen. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of PdAg NSs were measured to further confirm the surface composition and element valence. The XPS spectra of Ag 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 showed two peaks around 367.3 and 373.3 eV (Fig. 1E), indicating that all Ag was in the metallic form. The binding energies (BE) of 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 in the presence of Pd are about 334.6 and 339.8 eV (Fig. 1F), respectively, which also indicated that Pd existed mainly in the form of metal [36–39]. In summary, PdAg NSs was synthesized successfully.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was an effective method to evaluate changes in electrode surface resistance during sensor assembly. The EIS spectrum consisted of a semicircular part and a straight line part. The semicircular part corresponds to the electron transfer resistance (Ret) at higher frequencies, which can reflect the diffusion of the material on the electrode surface [40]. As shown in Fig. 2 A, curve a represented the Ret of the bare GCE. Compared to the bare electrode, the electrode modified by ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs showed a larger Ret (curve b) due to the poor conductivity of the donor material ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs. This demonstrated the successful modification of ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs. Then, when Ae (curve c) and BSA (curve d) were modified gradually on the ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs modified electrode, the conductivity of protein was poor, and electron transfer between GCE and solution was hindered, resulting in the resistance increasing. These results demonstrated the successful construction of the stepwise assembled immunosensor.

An immunosensor was constructed based on ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs and PdAg NSs, in which ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs was used as the donor for ECL-RET, PdAg NSs as the acceptor material, and K2S2O8 as the co-reactant. Under voltage excitation, ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs underwent a reduction reaction at the cathode to form ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs•−, and then S2O8 2- was reduced to SO4 •− at the cathode, and the two interacted to form excited ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs*, finally ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs* transition backed to the ground state to release energy in the form of light. Therefore, based on the above discussion and previous report [26], the luminescence principle was expressed as follows:
(1)

+ h ν

The feasibility of the immunosensor was explored. As shown in Fig. 2B, ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs were spread on the electrode surface as the donor, the antigen was connected to the base part, and the PdAg NSs were connected to the antibody as the probe part. When there was no 5-FU detection substance (curve I), the probe part was connected to the substrate part, and the electrochemiluminescence intensity was low. At this time, the energy generated by ox-MWCNT-IGQDs excitation could be quenched by PdAg NSs. On the contrary, when 5-FU was present (curve II), the electrochemiluminescence intensity was high. As shown in Fig. 2C, the UV absorption of PdAg NSs (curve i) and the ECL emission of ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs (curve ii) were tested. Comparing their spectra, it could be seen that the emission wavelength of ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs overlapped with the absorption wavelength of PdAg NSs, which proved the possibility of ECL-RET. The above showed that the assembled sensor was feasible for the detection of 5-fluorouracil.

The concentration of PdAg NSs acceptor, 5-FU coating antigen concentration, and antibody concentration had a great influence on the excellent performance of the ECL immunosensor. Therefore, an optimization experiment was carried out on the above conditions, and the results were shown in Fig. 3 . In the construction of ECL immunosensor, the amount of ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs donor material was a certain value, the concentration of acceptor was very critical to the quenching effect of the donor, the optimization experiment was carried out on the concentration of PdAg NSs acceptor. As shown in Fig. 3A, when the concentration of PdAg NSs was between 0.2 mg/mL and 0.8 mg/mL, the intensity of electrochemiluminescence signal reached its maximum value at 0.5 mg/mL. When the concentration of PdAg NSs exceeded 0.5 mg/mL, the ΔECL strength decreased rapidly. This is because the high concentration of PdAg NSs had a strong quenching effect on ox-MWCNTs-IGQDs.

The concentration of 5-FU coating antigen and antibody had a direct effect on the competitive immunization process. As shown in Fig. 3B, ΔECL signal intensity increased first and then decreased with the increase of 5-FU coating antigen concentration, and reached the peak at 8 μg/mL. This is because the increase of antigen concentration leaded to the enhancement of antibody specificity, and the binding of antigen enhanced the quenching effect, leading to the decrease of ΔECL signal. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 3C, ΔECL reached its maximum at 5 μg/mL antibody concentration and then began to decline. This is because high antibody concentrations reduced the competitiveness of immunosensor. Therefore, 8 μg/mL antigen and 5 μg/mL antibody were selected as the best conditions for constructing the immunosensor.

Under the above optimal detection conditions, the fabricated immunosensor was used for quantitative detection of 5-FU. As shown in Fig. 4 A, it can be clearly seen that, from left to right (a → h), the concentration of 5-FU detection substance increased continuously from 0.0005 ng/mL to 500 ng/mL, and the ECL intensity also increased continuously. When the 5-FU test substance was present, the test substance and the 5-FU coating antigen compete with limited antibodies, and the higher the test substance concentration, the less the number of PdAg NSs-Ab probes that specifically reacted with the coating antigen, resulting in a small quenching effect on ox-MWCNTs-GQDs and a higher ECL signal. What is more noteworthy was that when the concentration of 5-FU ranges from 0.0005 to 500 ng/mL, there was a good linear relationship between the ECL intensity (I) and the logarithm of the concentration of 5-FU (lg c). As can be seen from Fig. 4B, the linear regression equation of 5-FU detection was I = 966.1 lg c + 7291.1 and the linear correlation coefficient is 0.9973. The lowest detection limit of the constructed immunosensor for 5-FU was as low as 0.17 pg/mL (S/N = 3). In addition, the electrochemiluminescence immunosensor designed in this work and other test methods for the detection of 5-FU previously reported are listed in Table S1. By comparing the detection range and the minimum detection limit of different detection methods, it can be found the proposed immunosensor had a wider detection range and lower limit of detection than other methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability determination of the proposed immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the sensor is the key to determine its excellent performance. We selected eight substances that may cross-react with 5-fluorouracil (A): 5-bromouracil (B), thymine (C), dihydrouracil (D), 5-fluoro-1-(tetrahydro-2-furfuryl)uracil (E), cytosine (F), uridine (G), 5-fluoro-2′-deoxyuridine (H) and capecitabine (I), in which the concentration of 5-FU and interfering substances were 1 ng/mL. Table S2 listed the structural formula of 5-FU and the eight interfering substances. Fig. S3 showed that 5-fluoro-1-(tetrahydro-2-furfuryl)uracil, 5-fluoro-2′-deoxyuridine had crossover rates about 2.3 % and 1.5 %, respectively, due to their structure similar to 5-fluorouracil. The crossover rates of the other six interfering substances and 5-FU were almost zero, and there was no obvious crossover, indicating that the immunosensor had good selectivity.

The stability of the sensor is a key factor in practical applications, as shown in Fig. 5 A, as the concentration of 5-FU detector increased (0.001, 0.1, 10, 100 ng/mL), the ECL intensity also increased continuously. When the sensor was scanned in K2S2O8 solution for 5 consecutive cycles, the ECL signal intensity remained basically unchanged, and the relative standard deviation under each cycle was within 2.2 %, which indicated that the constructed immunosensor in this work had good stability. In addition, the sensor was tested in K2S2O8 solution for 18 consecutive cyclic scans, as shown in Fig. 5B, the ECL signal intensity remained stable and the RSD was only 1.9 %. It took about 10 min to complete a 5-FU detection. Above results show that the constructed ECL immunosensor has good stability, it will provide a reference for the practical application of ECL immunosensor.

The detection ability of the constructed ECL immunosensor in real samples was verified. We spiked human serum samples, and prepared 0.01 ng/mL, 0.1 ng/mL and 10 ng/mL spiked samples respectively. As shown in Table 1 , it was concluded that the recovery rate of this immunosensor for human serum samples was between 97.3 % and 112.8 %, which was within the acceptable range, the relative standard deviation was less than 12.3 %, indicating that the prepared immunosensor had good practical application value in detecting 5-FU in actual samples.

In this work, an ECL immunosensor based on ECL-RET between ox-MWCNTs-GQDs and PdAg NSs was constructed for the detection of 5-FU in human serum. On the one hand, the ox-MWCNTs-GQDs material with non-toxic, high luminous efficiency and good stability was used as the donor, and the activated material was connected to the antigen as the base part. PdAg NSs was used as acceptor materials, and the antibody was directly connected to the probe part. On the other hand, the broad UV absorption and the emission spectrum of ox-MWCNTs-GQDs achieved a greater degree of overlap. The immunosensor based on the high-efficiency ECL-RET system had a sensitive response to 5-FU in a wide linear range, and exhibited satisfactory results in real samples, which would provide possibility for the detection of 5-fluorouracil in patient serum.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We really appreciate the support from the Science Fund from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21175097, No. 31772053), the Project of Scientific and Technologic Infrastructure of Suzhou (SZS201207), the Supporting Funds for key research and development Project of the Ministry of Science and Technology (5010900122), the Suzhou Industry, State and Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Novel Functional Polymeric Materials (SYG201636) and the Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (No. YX10900212).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.108066.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
